article.title: TypeScript 6.0 Beta Marks the End of the JavaScript Era as Microsoft Prepares the Go-Powered 10x Faster Compiler
article.summary: Microsoft releases TypeScript 6.0 Beta, the final version written in JavaScript, modernizing defaults and clearing the path for TypeScript 7's Go-based rewrite targeting 10x faster builds.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

Microsoft released the TypeScript 6.0 Beta on February 11, 2026, marking a historic inflection point for one of the most widely used programming languages in the world. The release is explicitly framed as a transition release — the last version of TypeScript ever to be built on a JavaScript codebase. TypeScript 7.0, whose Go-based implementation is already publicly available for testing, is expected to deliver build times up to 10 times faster than today's compiler.

Installation is available immediately via `npm install -D typescript@beta`.

## What We Know

### The End of Self-Hosting

TypeScript has been written in itself — a pattern known as self-hosting or bootstrapping — since the language's inception more than a decade ago. As [Microsoft's official announcement notes](https://devblogs.microsoft.com/typescript/announcing-typescript-6-0-beta/), TypeScript's creator Anders Hejlsberg explained the decision to abandon this approach: "Since the inception of TypeScript more than a decade ago, TypeScript has been written in itself. That's brought a lot of benefits, but it's also consistently brought some challenges, in particular around performance and scalability."

The solution is a complete rewrite of the TypeScript compiler and language service in Go, a project internally known as "Project Corsa" and publicly tracked at [github.com/microsoft/typescript-go](https://github.com/microsoft/typescript-go) under the same open source license as TypeScript itself.

### Performance Benchmarks

The motivation for the shift becomes clear from the benchmarks Microsoft has shared for the native port, documented in the [TypeScript native port announcement](https://devblogs.microsoft.com/typescript/typescript-native-port/). On real-world codebases:

- The VS Code codebase (roughly 1.5 million lines of code) compiles in 7.5 seconds instead of 77.8 seconds — a 10.4x speedup.
- The Playwright codebase drops from 11.1 seconds to 1.1 seconds, a 10.1x improvement.
- TypeORM drops from 17.5 seconds to 1.3 seconds, a 13.5x gain.
- Editor startup time falls from approximately 9.6 seconds to 1.2 seconds across tested projects.
- Memory usage is projected to fall by approximately 50%.

These gains come from Go's ability to run native compiled code and leverage shared-memory multi-threading — capabilities that the JavaScript runtime cannot match at this scale.

### What Changes in 6.0

TypeScript 6.0 Beta modernizes a set of defaults that had accumulated technical debt over the language's twelve-year history. As [InfoQ reports](https://www.infoq.com/news/2026/02/typescript-6-released-beta/), the release affects how projects are configured out of the box:

- **`strict` is now `true` by default.** Projects that had not explicitly enabled strict mode will begin surfacing errors they were previously able to ignore.
- **`module` defaults to `esnext`**, replacing the older CommonJS default that no longer reflects the direction of the JavaScript ecosystem.
- **`target` defaults to `es2025`**, aligning TypeScript's output target with modern JavaScript environments.
- **`types` defaults to an empty array** (`[]`) instead of automatically importing every package under `node_modules/@types`. Microsoft reports that this single change produced 20–50% faster build times in tested projects, because the compiler no longer processes type definitions for packages that project code never references.
- **`rootDir` no longer infers automatically** from source file locations.

For teams that need time to migrate, all breaking defaults can be suppressed by adding `"ignoreDeprecations": "6.0"` to their `tsconfig.json`. This flag signals intent to migrate before TypeScript 7.0 removes the deprecated options entirely.

### New Language Features

Beyond the default changes, TypeScript 6.0 Beta ships several additions aligned with the advancing ECMAScript standard:

- **Temporal API types** (Stage 3 proposal): Built-in TypeScript types for the long-awaited JavaScript date/time API are now included.
- **`RegExp.escape()`** (now Stage 4, part of ES2026): TypeScript 6.0 adds full type support.
- **Map upsert methods**: `Map.getOrInsert()` and `Map.getOrInsertComputed()` (Stage 4) receive typed support.
- **Subpath imports**: Support for Node.js `#/` prefixes in import mappings, enabling cleaner module configuration without workaround patterns.
- **DOM library consolidation**: The `dom` library now bundles `dom.iterable` and `dom.asynciterable`, eliminating common configuration confusion.
- **Improved type inference**: Methods that do not reference `this` are no longer treated as contextually sensitive, matching the behavior of arrow functions and reducing spurious errors in common patterns.

### What Gets Deprecated

TypeScript 6.0 formally deprecates a set of options that will be **removed outright in TypeScript 7.0**. As documented in the [official beta announcement](https://devblogs.microsoft.com/typescript/announcing-typescript-6-0-beta/), the affected configurations include:

- `target: es5` — ES5 output targets are no longer supported.
- `--outFile` — Teams should migrate to dedicated bundlers such as Vite, esbuild, or webpack.
- `--moduleResolution node` — Teams should migrate to `nodenext` or `bundler`.
- `--baseUrl` — Path aliases should be expressed through `paths` mappings instead.
- AMD, UMD, and SystemJS module formats — These reflect a now-obsolete era of JavaScript module systems.
- `--downlevelIteration`, `--esModuleInterop false`, and `--moduleResolution classic`.

## What We Don't Know

The exact release date for the stable TypeScript 6.0 has not been announced. Microsoft's blog indicates that TypeScript 7.0 — the Go-based native port — is targeting a release shortly after 6.0 reaches stable, with the roadmap pointing to mid-2026 as a likely window.

The degree to which TypeScript 7.0 will maintain full feature parity with the current compiler at launch also remains an open question. The `typescript-go` repository is publicly accessible and accepting community testing, but Microsoft has not published a formal compatibility matrix. The `--stableTypeOrdering` flag introduced in 6.0 is designed to help developers identify behavior differences between the current and native implementations before they upgrade.

It is also unclear how tooling built on the current TypeScript Language Server Protocol implementation will behave when 7.0's rewritten language service ships. The native version is intended to migrate to a proper LSP architecture, which could require updates from IDE and editor plugin maintainers.

## Analysis

The scale of this transition is difficult to overstate. TypeScript is the lingua franca of large-scale JavaScript development — used at virtually every major technology company and embedded in the build tooling of frameworks including Angular, Next.js, Remix, and NestJS. The decision to rewrite the compiler in Go rather than in a newer systems language such as Rust drew initial scrutiny when it was first announced in early 2025, but Microsoft's reasoning centers on the structural similarity between Go's approach to concurrency and the existing TypeScript compiler's architecture, which made automated translation more tractable.

For individual developers, the 6.0 beta's most immediate consequence is the `strict: true` default. Teams with older codebases that had deferred strict mode adoption will now need to confront a backlog of suppressed type errors — though the `ignoreDeprecations` escape hatch buys time. For large enterprise projects, the migration window between 6.0 and 7.0 is likely to be a significant engineering effort.

The performance argument, however, is compelling. TypeScript's compile-time overhead has long been a friction point for developer experience at scale. If the Go port delivers on its 10x headline number across a broad range of projects, it would represent one of the most substantial improvements to JavaScript toolchain ergonomics in the language's history.
